data.title: After Recognising This, I've Decided To Go All In On BDCs
data.description:
The debate about BDC headwinds has shifted from interest rate risk to credit risk being as the main source for potential value destruction.

Starting from about mid-2025 almost everyone expected mounting BDC dividend cuts due to falling base rates. To some extent it has already materialized as many players have recently synchronized their dividend payouts with the new interest rate reality.

Yet, because of the oil price shock, incremental base rate cuts seem unrealistic this year. In fact, currently, we can see that the market has started to chat about potential rate hikes.

While this should, theoretically, provide a relief to BDCs (in the context of their dividend coverage), what we see is the complete opposite. Namely, the market has sent the BDC world (BIZD) into a heavy (almost distressed-like) zone.

And it makes perfect sense if the assumption is that there will be huge manifestation of credit risk.

The credit risk is more important and with more tangible consequences than the interest rate risk, which what really does is simply aligns BDC dividend yields to the lower-yield environment (while, actually, boosting the credit risk protection layer as the underlying companies can enjoy cheaper debt service).

Now, in my humble opinion, what does not make sense is the level of discount that has been currently assigned by the market.

The BDC median P/NAV is 0.73x - i.e., a discount of 27%.

How I see it is that the market has once again overreacted to a situation in which the direction of travel moves from positive to negative.

Sure, in the prevailing market situation, it would be only reasonable to expect higher default rates and thus some discounts to account for potential NAV declines.

Yet, the question is whether we should really expect "more than 3 standard deviation move from the mean".

I don't think so.

I don't see evidence of that, nor do I see similar precedents in the past, which include such periods as GFC, 2015/2016 crude oil shock, COVID-19 and rapidly surging interest rates in 2022.

What the market has assumed

I will try to flesh out in a simple/plain English what the market has baked into the cake for most BDCs.

In the table below I have gathered 8 select externally managed BDCs, which represent the overall sector quite well.

Among these we can find giants such as Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), core middle market focused names as Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) and some select "above average risk" picks as CION Investment Corporation (CION) and Crescent Capital BDC, Inc. (CCAP).

And just to quickly explain what some columns mean:

  • "P/NAV" and "Discount" reflect the level of discount at which each specific BDC trades now. The discount implies what portion of the reported NAV (shareholder equity) the market has questioned and potentially assumed to be largely written off.
  • "Leverage" reflects Q4 2025 debt to equity level.
  • "Unl. discount" adjusts the P/NAV discount for the leverage effects, which allows us to arrive an estimate on what share of total BDC assets are assumed by the market to be written off. Namely, the "Discount" column shows how discounted equity value is and the "Unl. discount" column shows how discounted the asset base is.
  • "Non-accrual" shows the Q4 2025 reported non-accrual rate based on FV (i.e., % of assets marked as underperforming and being subject to distress).
  • "Difference" shows at what multiple the current non-accrual rate should inflate for the market's discount to come to fruition.

All the data stem from Q4 2025 financial reports.

Ticker P/NAV Discount Leverage Unl. discount Non-accrual Difference
ARCC 0.91 9% 1.11 4% 1.2% 4x
BXSL 0.90 10% 1.29 4% 0.5% 9x
KBDC 0.86 14% 1.00 7% 1.4% 5x
OBDC 0.77 23% 1.25 10% 1.1% 9x
BBDC 0.76 24% 1.23 11% 0.2% 54x
MSDL 0.72 28% 1.19 13% 1.6% 8x
CCAP 0.69 31% 1.23 14% 2.0% 7x
CION 0.49 51% 1.59 20% 1.8% 11x
Median 0.73 27% 1.25 12% 1.9% 6x

Why this assumption is likely wrong

Now, let me put this into two perspectives.

The first one is what we could see above by looking at the column "Difference". For most of the current discounts to become justified the BDCs have to suddenly report at least 4-5x larger non-accruals as what they have done in Q4 2025.

Granted, the AI could disrupt their SaaS-linked portfolios and the inflationary headwinds might trigger new bankruptcies.

Yet, I am not sure that I can find anything in the market/economy that has changed in the past couple of months (or quarters) so dramatically that would substantiate these kinds of discounts.

This leads us now to the second perspective, which is more conceptual.

The historical non-accrual rate (before the COVID-19 period) is indeed higher than the current one:

But the difference is somewhere between 2x - 2.5x of what was reported in Q3 2025 (by assessing most BDC Q4 earnings and I can tell you that the situation has largely remained consistent with Q3).

Note, in COVID-19 period, when the risks and uncertainty were extremely high - nowhere close to what we have now - the peak non-accrual rate jumped to 5.2%. However, this is expressed on a cost basis, which means that on a FV basis the metric should be lower (~4.5% would be a reasonable assumption).

Fair point, if the assumption is that we are about to face another COVID-19 shock, sure, some select BDCs might be fairly valued.

Now, to make the matter a bit more complicated, but certainly worse for the bear case, we should factor in the recovery rates as well, which reflect what percentage of the non-accruals (written down debt) gets paid back in the bankruptcy and/or corporate liquidation process:

To be very conservative, we could adjust the non-accrual (or default) rate by two thirds to arrive at a final loss rate, which already reject the current discount justification under COVID-19 equivalent scenario.

And let me clarify why a 33% recovery rate truly conservative:

  • BDCs have never been as first-lien loan investment biased (the safest area in the capital structure) as they are now.
  • On average BDCs invest in portfolio companies, which carry ~50% LTV capital structures, which mean that more than half company of asset value has to vanish for the BDC to recognize loss of loan principal.

Plus, let's keep in mind that these are just averages.

For example, ARCC which is the largest publicly traded BDC out there with a notable chunk of loans placed below senior secured first lien lever has since its inception (2004) recorded an average annualized realized loss rate of 0.1%.

Moreover, the average annual loss rate (2017-2025) for BDCs stands only at 0.48%:

In a nutshell, for the current BDC discounts to get fully justified, a major blow-out has to take place. The situation has to be much worse than in the COVID-19 period, which was, arguably, more volatile and risky time as during GFC drama.

The reality is that most BDCs are stronger than ever (e.g., tighter underwriting standards, lower PIK, higher share of first-lien loans, more diversified/larger etc).

The fact that the non-accruals are at their historical lows in combination with the increasingly shaky economy and rising risks for select SaaS businesses my base case is that we will see some expansion of non-accruals.

Yet, even if they double or triple, the consequences (adjusted for recovery rates) would not come even close to what the market has currently baked into the cake.

Action plan

This is my action plan, which might serve as a basis of inspiration in terms of how or in what direction durable NAV (and income) BDC portfolios could be calibrated.

Personally, I have decided to direct all of my incremental savings into BDCs until the total BDC exposure reaches 15%. I will do this on a dollar-cost averaging basis by committing fixed sums of money each month for next 4 to 6 month period.

There are five pillars, which underpin my BDC allocation strategy.

  1. Below average PIK and OID. The idea is to avoid BDCs, which derive their income from non-cash sources in a material way. Anything that goes above 10% is, in my view, already speculative (not necessarily bad, but definitely required higher discounts).
  2. Prudent leverage profile. I want to avoid BDC, which are in a position from which they have to focus on deleveraging, which, in turn, could lead to NII reduction. BDCs with debt-to-equity of 1.1x or below could instead utilize some fresh debt capacity to take advantage of times like these when the spreads are elevated due to the huge scepticism about BDCs.
  3. Structurally low non-accruals. I don't want to take chances by venturing into BDCs, which have high non-accruals with an idea that going forward they could work them down and thus unlock sizeable returns. The focus is on high-quality BDCs with low non-accruals now and in the past.
  4. Conservative (high margin of safety) LTVs and interest coverage. This is simple. My focus in on BDCs, which have invested in companies that have LTVs and interest coverage ratios below 50% and above 2x, respectively.
  5. Equity gain potential. BDCs that carry equity investment in their portfolios have the ability to monetize these stakes, which oftentimes come with sizeable gain. The equity-like gains could be then either distributed in juicy supplemental dividends or used as an offset to credit losses.

For the BDCs which I reflected in the table above, my clear favorite is KBDC (see more details in my ticker specific piece). KBDC ticks all of the aforementioned boxed except for the fifth one - the equity part. However, this, in my view, is nicely compensated with the fact that KBDC has just 2% of its portfolio located in potentially struggling SaaS names.

Apart from KBDC, the other two BDC, which form my Top 3 list are Capital Southwest (CSWC) and Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) - see my ticker-specific articles here and here, respectively. Both of them tick all of the necessary boxes. And an interesting fact: recently, they both announced a joint partnership for expanding their portfolios further.

CWSC is an internally managed lower middle market focused BDC with around 10% of its portfolio placed in high-quality equity investments. Because of the LMM exposure, CSWC has been able to underwrite loan investments at much stricter terms than what we typically can find in, say, the upper middle market. It has a high-quality portfolio that is underpinned by a prudent balance sheet (debt to equity of 1.06x).

TRIN is also an internally managed BDC, but with a more diversified portfolio exposure than CSWC. It lends across five verticals: equipment finance, tech lending, life sciences and sponsor finance. TRIN has one of the most robust portfolios (in terms of quality) in the entire BDC space. For example, the PIK accounts for ~1% of the total income and the non-accruals consume less than 1% of the total portfolio asset base (on a FV basis).

In a nutshell, KBDC, CSWC and TRIN are my Top 3 BDC picks to which I will be feeding most of my incremental portfolio savings until the total portfolio BDC exposure reaches the 17% mark.

data.title: Tower Semiconductor: Pure-Play Israeli Foundry In A Boom Cycle
data.description:
Tower Semiconductor (TSEM), which is up a staggering 335% since my last analysis of the company, is being re-rated from a speciality foundry into an optical AI infrastructure name. This is good for the company, but the equity is discounting future silicon photonics earnings well before they are visible in reported results. This makes the investment risky right now, even if the underlying company is doing exceptionally well and positioned in a high-growth vector.

Oriole Collaboration

On March 16, Tower formed a collaboration with Oriole to commercialize nanosecond optical circuit switching on Tower's silicon photonics platform. This is for AI scaling up and scaling out. Oriole's PRISM Ultra claims photonic fabrics with 180ns xPU latency, as well as one-hop connectivity to up to 1M nodes, and much lower switching power. Therefore, this could make Tower a core manufacturing layer behind an emerging AI network stack.

Tower now has public optical ties across Nvidia (NVDA) 1.6T modules, Salience optical circuit switches, Scintil DWDM lasers, Oriole fabrics, and Coherent's (COHR) 400G/lane path to 3.2T optics. This shows great breadth and depth and reveals that TSEM is certainly not a one-off release-spike stock. The main constraint is valuation, not company positioning or trajectory.

To outline just how powerful the optical opportunity is, consider SiGe plus silicon photonics reached $421M in 2025 (27% of revenue), versus $241M in 2024. Silicon photonics alone rose to $228M from $106M.

Market Share & Visibility

With the opportunity and strong positioning addressed, we still have to acknowledge that Tower is small in the global foundry industry. For example, TrendForce put it No. 7 by Q4 2025 revenue. However, management says it is the primary provider and by far the majority supplier of 1.6T silicon PICs. Therefore, Tower's competitive advantage is not overall dominance, but front-running a very valuable niche.

Visibility is unusually strong for a ramp that is this early. Tower has lifted SiGe/SiPho capex to $920M and management is targeting qualification by Q4 of 2026, then starting in 2027. Management also says that more than 70% of total silicon photonics capacity through 2028 is reserved or in process, backed by prepayments.

Also, the build-out model is aggressive, with $2.84B of revenue, a 39.4% gross margin, and $750M net profit at mature utilization. This excludes any Intel Fab 11 contribution. The market is clearly underwriting that future well ahead of realization.

Market Constraints

Even though the optical AI boom is here and strong, adoption is still uneven for now. TrendForce still expects copper to remain dominant for ultra-short in-rack links through at least 2028. But co-packaged optics may be only ~0.5% of data-center transceiver modules in 2026 before rising toward 35% by 2030.

Tower's nearest monetization is pluggables and 1.6T transceivers sold through module makers and integrators rather than direct Nvidia shipments. Management is still framing co-packaged optics as an incremental future opportunity (not quite yet the current earnings engine). This brings us nicely onto valuation, where it is clear that investors have become over-exuberant about the stock.

Valuation

I think it makes sense to start with the valuation grades here, because as you can see they are all deeply in the red, including the most important reading of all for a high-growth name, the forward PEG. At 205% higher than the sector median, I would advise against entering the equity at this juncture. It is too expensive for rational capital allocation. I can't call this the top based on the unpredictability of sentiment, but the valuation is not ripe for entry. Therefore, I am staying out for now.

The technicals show a similar story. The daily RSI has breached well above the overbought threshold, and the 50-day moving average is sitting well below the present price. This reflects bullish momentum, but when coupled with the forward PEG as high as it is, prudence recommends caution.

From a consensus growth standpoint, it's clear why the investment is popular. Six analysts on consensus are forecasting an average of 62% year-over-year growth in 2027. If it weren't for the valuation constraints, this would be a high-alpha-producing stock indeed.

If the company achieves December 2027 normalized EPS of $5 and the forward P/E de-rates to a more moderate 40, the stock price will be $200 in December 2026. This reflects a 13% upside, but implies bullish sentiment. If sentiment normalizes, returns could certainly be negative. Hence my Hold rating.

Other Risks

Firstly, we must give credence to competition, which is rising fast. GlobalFoundries (GFS) bought AMF and is now saying it is the largest pure-play silicon photonics foundry by revenue. In Tower's 2025 20-F, the company names GlobalFoundries (GFS), Vanguard, DongBu, X-Fab, Hua Hong, TSMC (TSM), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC), and SMIC as competitors. It also competes with integrated device manufacturers that allocate capacity to foundry operations.

Operational risk is also present as always, and becomes more acute given that the valuation is presently unforgiving. The bull case depends on tool deliveries, qualification, yields, high-temperature operating life, broader reliability, and customer sign-off across a 5x capacity build.

Conclusion: Hold

Tower Semiconductor is the kind of investment you wish you bought a year ago, but now, it indeed looks a little late in the game to enter the trade. Instead, the wise move would be to keep the company on a watchlist, given its positioning in high-growth tech, and to wait for a more suitable entry point if one occurs in the future.

I never like it when an equity has a forward PEG ratio that's excessively higher than the sector median. Over 200% higher is always a no-go for me. The only reason my rating is not Sell here is that I believe the company has a strong operational positioning, and over the long term, the stock's fundamentals can catch up to the price. However, such a strategy is not a reliable way to produce alpha.

data.title: A Real-Time Indicator On The Warning Track
data.description:
Despite encouraging words from the Trump administration about negotiations with Iranian officials, the major market indexes surrendered approximately half of the prior day's gains. Oil prices climbed, and Treasury yields failed to ease and confirm the optimism displayed in the stock market. Additionally, investors continue to expect a rate hike by the Federal Reserve as soon as its next meeting in April. I think there is zero chance for a rate hike in April, and it's very unlikely we will see one this year, as the consensus of Fed officials still has one rate cut penciled in for this year as of their most recent Summary of Economic Projections, which was updated on March 18.

A rate hike would do little to lower oil prices and take months to actually have an effect. Furthermore, the best cure for high oil prices is high oil prices, because if it sustains, it slows growth and reduces demand. We are going to see the rate of inflation tick higher in the months ahead as higher energy costs filter through the economy, but I think the roll-off of tariffs from the annualized number during the second half of the year will help to stem a significant rise in the both inflation gauges. If we do see a rate cut later this year, as I expect, it will probably be to address continued softening in the labor market. I am more concerned about growth than inflation as we navigate the economic impact from the ongoing geopolitical crisis.

My favorite real-time economic indicator is the flash S&P Global Composite PMI (Purchasing Managers Index), because it accounts for approximately 85% of the survey responses from some 650 manufacturing companies and 500 service providers collected during the month of March. This is the first opportunity to see how the economy is responding to the war in Iran. It shows that we are still in expansion but also clearly on the warning track, as we were last April.

The index fell from 51.9 in February to 51.4 in March, with 50 being the line of demarcation between expansion and contraction. As one would assume, business activity slowed to its lowest level since the President's reciprocal tariffs were announced and shortly thereafter reduced, while prices paid for inputs and other materials rose. My expectation is that we see a similar rebound in the index as the war in Iran de-escalates.

The good news is that the manufacturing sector continued to revive with strength in new orders and production, as tariff concerns eased following the Supreme Court's ruling and expected tariff refunds. The bad news is that the more important service sector saw growth slow to an 11-month low, as export orders slowed sharply. Additionally, the prices paid for materials and other inputs spiked higher, rising at the fastest rate in 10 months, resulting in selling prices increasing at the fastest rate in more than three years. We saw a preview of this in last month's Producer Price Index. As a result of the deterioration in sentiment, employment fell for the first time in a year.

No wonder the Trump administration sees a sense of urgency to end this conflict. Again, the economy is on the warning track as it was last April. S&P Global's Chief Economist, Chris Williamson, noted that the index is consistent with GDP growth of just 1.3% in the first quarter and a rate of inflation approaching 4%. An exit strategy from this quagmire will be more difficult than it was last spring when all the president had to do was rescind his punitive tariffs. This situation is far more complex considering all the parties involved. I still believe that the strength of the economy in advance of the conflict will allow it to absorb the temporary shock from higher energy prices and return to sub-trend growth. Markets look forward and typically bottom well before geopolitical conflicts like this one end. The keys are to see oil prices gradually decline and Treasury yields ease, confirming any rebound in stock prices.

data.title: Micron Says Go Big Or Go Home As It Ramps Up Capex Far More Than Peers
data.description:
Performance assessment

Micron (MU) has massively outperformed the broader stock market index since my last update on the stock:

Elevator pitch thesis

Here's how I'm looking at Micron now:

  1. Micron is seeing a demand bonanza and securing a better revenue quality profile
  2. Gross margins are at record levels, but it may not be sustainable
  3. High greenfield capex spending elevates the risk profile
  4. A contracted valuation multiple with massive earnings growth expectations is attractive
  5. The charts say hold on, but don't buy more now

Micron is seeing a demand bonanza and securing a better revenue quality profile

In the semiconductors industry, AI and traditional server demand are severely constrained by a lack of adequate DRAM and NAND supply. This has led spot DRAM and NAND market prices to increase 5.5x and nearly 4x over the last 6 months. This is leading to a massive demand and price-hike bonanza for Micron in its DRAM and NAND segments:

The chart below showing dwindling inventory levels clearly confirms the narrative of memory chip shortages:

Memory is becoming increasingly more essential as compute demand increases, giving Micron a wonderful long-term demand tailwind:

As AI evolves, we expect compute architectures to become more memory intensive. This is why we strongly believe that Micron is one of the biggest beneficiaries and enablers of AI. AI hasn't just increased demand for memory, it has fundamentally recast memory as a defining strategic asset in the AI era.

- CEO Sanjay Mehrotra in the Q2 FY26 earnings call

The current memory market shortages are expected to grow bigger for the rest of this year, with pricing projections of a further 60% increase. This is leading to an explosion in revenue growth expectations:

As customers scramble to secure long-term memory supply, Micron is benefiting by locking in multi-year supply agreements via Strategic Customer Agreements (SCAs). This differs from typical long-term agreements (LTAs), which tend to be one-year contracts:

SCA is multi-year agreement... LTAs have tended to be typically 1-year agreement... in this environment of extremely tight supply outlook in the foreseeable timeframe as well... our customers are very motivated in order for their own planning purposes and for their better predictability to have these structural strategic agreements with us... these agreements are really meant to bring stability and greater visibility into our business model as well.

- CEO Sanjay Mehrotra in the Q2 FY26 earnings call

Overall, I believe this improves the quality of Micron's revenue profile as visibility is increased and volatility is reduced.

Gross margins are at record levels, but it may not be sustainable

Micron's gross profit margins are on a tear, and next quarter's guidance suggests a further increase to above 80%:

These increases are driven by higher memory prices and a favorable mix of memory products:

This improvement was driven primarily by higher pricing and also included favorable mix and cost performance. Fiscal Q2 gross margin nearly doubled from a year ago and was a company record... We expect higher price, lower cost and favorable mix to all contribute to gross margin expansion in Q3.

- CFO Mark Murphy in the Q2 FY26 earnings call

The market seems to find it harder to grasp that this trend of gross margin expansion as the gap between the consensus estimates vs actual guidance has widened over the last couple of quarters:

That said, there are some growing concerns that the company may be at peak margins and that the next trajectory may be a reversion back down about 1000 to 2000 bps. This is driven by the fact that, according to some indications from semiconductor names such as Applied Materials (AMAT), incremental demand for memory chips such as NAND may be lower than expected:

NAND spot continues to benefit from anticipated increase in DC products (eSSDs, NVDA KV cache offload, etc.), though per AMAT demand created from KV cache could only be single-digit % of overall NAND TAM. Similarly, MU FQ3 [gross margin] guide of 81.0% could be near peak cycle, eventually stabilizing toward 60-70% historical high prior to AI.

- BoFA Analyst Vivek Arya

DC stands for data center, and eSSDs refer to enterprise SSDs.

KV stands for key-value. Large AI models do a lot of KV lookups, especially in inference workloads. A KV cache offload refers to moving that KV data out of the GPUs and into cheaper memory pieces such as SSDs and NAND devices. This reduces GPU memory bottlenecks but at the cost of some latency.

High greenfield capex spending elevates the risk profile

Micron is very aggressively investing in new capacity across the board to bridge the large gap between high demand and limited supply:

In DRAM, earlier this week, we announced the successful closing of the acquisition of the Tongluo site from Powerchip Semiconductor, completing the transaction ahead of schedule. We expect this site to support meaningful product shipments from the existing fab beginning in fiscal 2028... we plan to begin construction of a similar-sized second cleanroom at this site by the end of fiscal 2026. We continue to expect initial wafer output at our first Idaho fab in mid-calendar 2027, and ground preparation has begun for our second Idaho fab. We broke ground on our first fab at the New York site, and initial ground preparation activities are ahead of plan. In Japan, we are making good progress on ground preparation for our cleanroom expansion to enable future technology transitions in our Hiroshima site...

- CEO Sanjay Mehrotra in the Q2 FY26 earnings call

From a numbers perspective, Micron has raised FY26 (Aug 2026 fiscal year end) capex guidance up from $20B to $25B, primarily due to expansion of their newly acquired Tongluo fab site in Taiwan from Powerchip Semiconductor (TWSE:6770) and US fab projects. In the year after, capex spend is expected to increase another $10B from greenfield construction-related expansions in the US and other parts of the world too:

To give a sense of perspective, Micron is going full throttle on capacity expansions compared to its peers. Just compare its 81% increase in FY26 capex spend vs peers:

Even though this increased spending seems to be well supported by an overflow of demand right now, greenfield (new project) expansions at a rate far higher than sectoral peers make me a little queasy. Excess capex spending in upcycles is a perennial risk in any capital-intensive industry. I've even warned of this risk in Mag 7 stocks such as Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG).

A contracted valuation multiple with massive earnings growth expectations is attractive

Micron is trading at a mere 3.17x 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA. This represents a 34% discount to its longer term median multiple of 4.8x:

The most bullish aspect about the valuation analysis is the fact that underlying EBITDA growth expectations are exploding, whilst the valuation multiples are contracting:

This results in an attractive valuation setup wherein a fast-growing MU stock is on sale at discounted valuations. It reminds me of the situation in Nvidia (NVDA) 3 years ago when its stock also exploded and moved seemingly parabolically. Yet, there was no undue overhype as the valuation multiples actually got cheaper, whilst the underlying earnings growth expectations exploded:

The charts say hold on, but don't buy more now

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

MU Technical Analysis

MU stock is in a strong uptrend, but the prices have been currently stuck in a range for the past couple of months. Due to this market state, the charts do not indicate a strong directional bias yet for initiating new buys. But at the same time, I believe it makes sense to hold on to existing buys.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Neutral as the powerful memory demand tailwinds are offset somewhat by the risks of peak gross margins and very aggressive capex spends
  • Valuation: Bullish due to a discounted valuation multiple and increased earnings growth expectations
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral due to a range-bound prices outlook
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Neutral, Hold

data.title: Progress Software Q1 Earnings Preview: Slowing Down Operations Is Concerning
data.description:
Introduction

Progress Software (PRGS) is due to report its Q1 numbers on the 30th of March after the market closes, so I wanted to cover what to expect and what I’ll be looking out for in the upcoming report, which I don’t think is going to surprise us numbers-wise, but the conference call might be quite interesting.

What to expect from Progress Software's Q1 earnings

Analysts’ expectations for earnings are around $1.57 adjusted and $0.51 on a GAAP basis, on $246.4m in revenue. This revenue figure translates to around 2.5% decline sequentially and 3.5% growth y/y. Quite lackluster growth expectations from the analysts, but how well do they stack up to what the management guided for the upcoming quarter? In the last quarter, which was Q4 ’25, the management gave a revenue range of $244m-$250m, so $247m at the midpoint, and an adjusted EPS range of $1.56-$1.62, $1.59 at the midpoint. It seems that the analysts are a bit more conservative on both fronts. It also seems that the analysts have been underestimating PRGS’s potential for a while, as in the last ten quarters, PRGS only missed the latest revenue estimates, while beating on EPS 100% of the time.

I wouldn’t be surprised to see the company beating on all fronts once again, as it seems to me that the management may be talking down on the company’s potential and providing a conservative estimate on purpose, so they can easily beat expectations.

First quarters of the year are usually the quiet ones for software companies like PRGS. I wouldn’t pay too much attention to the sequential decline in the top line and would put more emphasis on the company’s ARR numbers. The decline in sequential revenue could most likely just mean that most of the companies that they do business with already did their budget in Q4, which is always the busiest time for renewals. I’m not expecting any sort of surprises on the top and bottom lines out of the upcoming results.

What I’m looking for

We can see that the company did not avoid the SaaSpocalypse that hit pretty much every “AI software” name out there. If your company tracks ARR, focuses on automation, and digital experience, I could say with relatively high confidence that the share price of that company did not do very well recently. No matter the fundamentals, the AI valuation-reset narrative hit all AI-automation software companies.

I am going to look out for some sort of a reversal in this trend going forward and will be looking out for any indication from the management regarding the outlook for 2026.

The best place to look for this reversal will be the main performance indicator, which is ARR. In Q3, ARR grew 47% to $849m, and in Q4, the company lost its momentum when ARR grew only 2% to $852m. I am expecting this to start picking back up since there is a lot of AI momentum now that we know the hyperscalers are going to shovel hundreds of billions more dollars into their AI dreams this year, and I don’t expect it to change in 2027 either, given how constrained the memory capacity is for 2027 and beyond. But if we don’t see a meaningful improvement in ARR, it could mean we either see a maturation stage for PRGS, or the AI software business is just too saturated and someone else is starting to eat its lunch.

Also, I would like to see its net retention rate hold steady at 100% as it has in the past. A decline, coupled with a decline in ARR, could mean that there is too much competition, and that would be worrisome, which would certainly affect the company’s share price negatively.

Additionally, I would like to see how the company’s margins perform in a quieter quarter. So far, the company has achieved the rule of 40 by showing at least 18% top-line growth in 2025, coupled with an adjusted operating margin of 38%, but the upcoming quarter shows only around 3.5% top-line growth, so I am curious to see what the margins will look like there. It is cutting close.

Lastly, I am curious to hear if the management is going to talk about the takeover speculation that appeared to have negatively impacted its share price on the day of the announcement. It seems that a private equity firm, Thoma Bravo, is looking to take PRGS private, or some other player that is also part of the talks. The earnings conference call is a perfect time to announce something like this or dispel these rumors completely. I’m sure analysts on the call will most certainly mention this elephant in the room. Usually, when such speculation circles a company, it’s a positive for the share price, but this time it seemed not to be. Unusual.

Closing Comments

I am not interested in another AI play at the moment, and given the fierce competition, I would like to see what PRGS can do to overcome these ARR challenges, get even more profitable, and win additional clients, but that seems to be up in the air for now. Operations are slowing down, it seems, so I would like to see what the management can do to regain investors’ confidence going forward, or will they surrender to a takeover? My hold recommendation stands.

data.title: Hope Lifts Stocks And Bonds
data.description:
There continues to be hope in the capital and commodity markets that a US-Israel war on Iran can be brought to a conclusion soon. While news reports indicate that the US had drafted a 15-point peace plan that has been delivered to Iran via Pakistan, other reports indicate that the US is sending more people and weapons to the region. Iran has reportedly not yet formally responded to the proposal and has continued missile and drone attacks in the region. Meanwhile, a few more ships appear to have transited the Strait of Hormuz.

While the market has jumped back into risk assets at the little glimmers of hope along the way of a short conflict, the derivatives market continues to price in seemingly hawkish central bank policy responses, even if not as extreme as last week. The dollar (DXY) was sold in a flurry of activity late in North America yesterday, but follow-through has been limited today and the greenback is trading with a firmer bias. Equities and bonds are recovering, while oil prices (WTI and Brent) are pinned in narrow ranges near yesterday’s lows.

Prices

G10

• Yesterday, the euro surrendered about half of the gains posted on Tuesday, when many latched on to the “five-day grace period” seemingly granted by President Trump as a sign of progress toward ending the conflict. Yesterday the market was less sure. However, late in the session, a new wave of optimism washed across the markets, and in thin, late North American trading, the euro spiked to new session highs, slightly shy of $1.1630. Monday’s high was closer to $1.1640. It is consolidating today, mostly between $1.1585 and $1.1630. The $1.16 area may still be a battleground. Options for 1.8 bln euros expire at $1.1605 today. Another pile for nearly 1.4 bln euros expires at $1.16 tomorrow.

• For the third consecutive session, the dollar held above the previous session’s low against the Japanese yen yesterday and is trying to extend it today. The greenback recorded an inside day yesterday. It is trading quietly today and has been capped near JPY159.20. The market does not appear to have given up on the JPY160 area. There are $885 mln of options that expire at JPY160 today.

• Sterling gave up a little more than half of Monday’s gains yesterday and found bids above $1.3350. It peaked around $1.3445 in the flurry of activity in late North American turnover yesterday. It is trading inside yesterday’s range so far today and is in the narrowest range in nearly two weeks: ~$1.3370-1.3435. Options for GBP1.14 bln at $1.3350 expire tomorrow.

• The US dollar reached CAD1.3785 yesterday, a two-month high, and where options for $422 mln expire today. It is bid in European turnover and reached almost CAD1.3800. We have been targeting the CAD1.38 area, which houses the 200-day moving average and the (50%) retracement of the greenback’s decline from last November’s high (~CAD1.4140). Options for $590 mln expire at CAD1.38 today. A convincing push above there could target CAD1.3850.

• The Australian dollar fell for the third consecutive session and is extending it into the fourth session today, although it is inside yesterday’s range. It settled below $0.7000 for the second consecutive session. It recovered almost back to $0.7000 late yesterday after initially being sold slightly through $0.6940. Monday’s low was near $0.6910. Options for A$1.4 bln expire at $0.6950 today, and the low so far is about $0.6960. A break of the $0.6890-0.6900 area would inflict technical damage that could spur at least another cent decline.

EM

• The Mexican peso consolidated yesterday and enjoyed a slightly firmer close amid the late US dollar retreat. The dollar had already begun coming off the late NY morning high and thin dealings after the NY markets closed; the greenback was sold to new session lows near MXN17.70. It has edged a little lower today. Since the war began, the dollar has poked above MXN18.00 on an intraday basis twice, but both times, for different reasons, the dollar came off and settled closer to session lows. The 20-day moving average is around MXN17.6660, and the greenback has not settled below it since the war began.

• The dollar posted an inside day against the yuan yesterday. The dollar traded in wider intraday ranges in the first week or so of the war, but for nearly two weeks now has been broadly range-bound ~CNH6.860-6.92). It remains within Monday’s range so far today (~CNH6.8775-6.9185). The five- and 20-day moving averages of the dollar against the onshore yuan have converged a little above CNY6.89. The PBOC fixed the dollar at CNY6.8911 (CNY6.8943 yesterday).

• The Indian rupee fell to a record low today. Intervention by the RBI failed to prevent the US dollar from edging to a marginal new high against the rupee near INR93.9835. Global asset managers have liquidated around $11 bln of Indian equities this month. Bonds in the international indices have also experienced record outflows. Month-end importer dollar demand also has been reported.

Other Markets

• Although US indices finished lower yesterday, hope that a ceasefire can soon be agreed upon in the war on Iran is helping lift equities today. Nearly all the markets in the Asia-Pacific region rallied earlier today, with most of the large markets up more than 1%. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up for the third day, which, if sustained, would be the longest advance in more than a month. US index futures (SPX) are up 0.8-1.0%.

• Bonds are rallying. While the 10-year JGB yield fell by less than a basis point, the yield fell 9-11 bp in Australia and New Zealand. Benchmark yields are off mostly 5-8 bp in Europe, and peripheral spreads have narrowed. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off almost four basis points to 4.32%.

• Gold is trading at new highs for the week. It reached a little above $4600 after bottoming Monday slightly below $4100. The next technical target may be around $4685. Silver approached a four-day high near $74.55 and is consolidating now closer to $72.50. Monday’s low was near $61.

• May WTI is confined to an extremely narrow range today (~$86.60-89.55). The week’s low was recorded Monday near $84.35. The 20-day moving average is around $85.60, and the May contract has not traded below it since the war began.

Data

• The US February import and export prices seem dated now since the war, but import prices likely turned higher on a year-over-year basis, while the base effect warns that export price increases may have slowed, rising 2.6% in January. They peaked at 3.9% last September. Q4 '25 current account deficit is also due. It may be the smallest in a few years. For the year as a whole, it may be smaller than 2024, but it will still be the second largest on record. The report tends to have little market impact.

• After falling by 0.5% in January, the UK consumer price index rose by 0.4% in February. Yet, the base effect kept the year-over-year pace steady at 3.0%. The core rate ticked up to 3.2% from 3.1%. Service price inflation slowed to a still-elevated 4.3% from 4.4%. The swaps market is discounting about a 70% chance of a hike next month. At the end of last week, it was seen as almost 80% probability.

• Germany’s IFO survey showed weaker sentiment. The current assessment and expectations softened in March, and the overall business climate assessment stands at 86.4 (88.4 in February, revised from 88.6), the weakest since last February.

• Australia’s CPI was flat in February, and the year-over-year rate edged down to 3.7% from 3.8%. The trimmed mean rose by 0.2%, leaving the year-over-year rate steady at 3.3% given the revision from 3.4% initially. The Reserve Bank of Australia raised rates twice this year, and before the war on Iran started, the futures market was discounting another hike fully and almost a 50% chance of yet another. The market peaked on Monday, with three hikes fully discounted for the remainder of the year. Now, the futures market has two hikes fully discounted and about a 25% chance of a third.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Digital India: The Next Phase
data.description:
India's digital economy is shifting from IT exports to domestic consumption. DGIN's index rebalances toward fintech, e-commerce, and telecom to capture that growth.

The MVIS Digital India Index — the underlying benchmark for the VanEck Digital India ETF (DGIN) — is updating its methodology to better reflect where growth is occurring in India today, shifting exposure away from global IT exporters and toward domestic consumption, financial services, and connectivity. This piece outlines the structural case for India's domestic digital economy and explains what is driving the index change.

From Export IT to Domestic Digital

India's listed equity market has long been dominated by large IT services companies that generate the majority of their revenue from clients outside the country. For years, these exporters shaped India's representation in global equity indices. That weighting, however, no longer reflects the composition of India's evolving domestic economy.

Rising smartphone penetration and household income growth are accelerating domestic digital activity across payments, e-commerce, online financial services, and telecom infrastructure. India's GDP has expanded from roughly $2 trillion in 2015 to approximately $3.9 trillion in 20251, with a growing share of economic activity flowing through domestic digital channels. The companies capturing that growth are increasingly different from those that have historically defined India's listed market.

Structural Drivers

Three structural factors underpin the expansion of India's domestic digital economy.

  • Connectivity: India has approximately 958 million active internet users. A nationwide 5G rollout has extended high-speed access to urban and semi-urban markets, while data costs remain among the lowest globally.2 Together, this infrastructure enables digital businesses to reach consumers across income levels and geographies at low marginal cost.
  • Demographics: India's median age is 28, and its working-age population is large and expanding. Rising disposable incomes are translating into increased spending on digital services across finance, retail, entertainment, and consumer categories.
  • Financialization of savings: Indian households have historically concentrated savings in bank deposits, real estate, and gold. Digital brokerage platforms and fintech networks are changing that, reducing the cost and complexity of accessing capital markets. The number of individual equity investors has grown from roughly 30 million in 2019 to more than 120 million by 20253— a fourfold increase in six years. The result is a broader, deeper domestic investor base — one that provides structural support for India's expanding corporate sector.

Recent and Upcoming Listings

India's equity markets are beginning to reflect this structural shift. In 2025, four domestically focused digital companies — Meesho, Groww, Lenskart, and PhysicsWallah — listed on Indian exchanges, collectively representing more than $17 billion in estimated market capitalization at IPO. Among the companies expected to list in 2026, Reliance Jio Platforms and Flipkart represent two of the most anticipated listings in Indian market history. Collectively, this pipeline is expanding the investable universe of India's domestic digital economy and increasing the weight of domestically oriented businesses within listed equities.

Listed in 2025

Company Theme Est. Market Value at IPO
Meesho E-commerce ~$6B
PhysicsWallah EdTech ~$2-3B
Lenskart Direct-to-consumer retail ~$4B
Groww E-commerce ~$5B

Expected in 2026

Company Theme Est. Market Value at IPO
PhonePe Digital payments ~$12-15B
Zepto E-commerce ~$50B+
Reliance Jio Platforms Telecom / digital ecosystem ~$100B+
Flipkart E-commerce ~$60-70B
OYO E-travel ~$800M
SBI Mutual Fund E-investments ~$1.2B
Hero FinCorp E-loans ~$1.8B

Source: Company disclosures, press reports. 2026 market values are estimates. 2025 values reflect approximate IPO-period valuations. Expected listings are subject to market conditions and may not occur. Not intended as recommendations or investment advice.

Index Methodology Update

The MVIS Digital India Index is implementing a rules-based methodology change designed to realign the index with the current shape of India's digital economy. Under the updated rules, companies must generate more than 50% of their revenues from domestic sources to be eligible for inclusion. In practice, this reduces weight in IT services and consulting companies — primarily large-cap exporters whose revenues are tied to global IT spending — while increasing exposure to communication services, financials, and consumer discretionary companies that serve India's domestic market.

Concretely, the update reduces weight in names such as Infosys and Wipro, and increases weight in telecom infrastructure, digital payments, and consumer platform companies. The result is an index more directly aligned with the structural growth drivers outlined above — connectivity, rising incomes, and the financialization of household savings.

Sector Exposure: Before and After

Information Technology declines from 44% to 7%. Communication Services, Financials, and Consumer Discretionary collectively increase from approximately 44% to 79%.

Sector Current (%) Old (%)
Communication Services 30.6 17.6
Financials 26.2 12.7
Consumer Discretionary 22.5 13.9
Energy 8.0 8.0
Information Technology 7.0 44.0
Industrials 5.6 3.3
Health Care 0.0 0.5

Source: MarketVector Indices. Data as of 3/12/2026. Index composition is not directly representative of fund holdings. Not intended as recommendations or investment advice.

Thematic Exposure: Before and After

IT services and consulting declines from 40% to under 2%. E-commerce and telecommunications combined represent nearly 70% of the updated index.

Theme Current (%) Old (%)
E-commerce (incl. online financial services) 36.3 21.1
Telecommunications services and infrastructure 32.4 22.3
Electronic payment processing 11.0 3.6
Hardware and communications equipment 10.9 4.4
Internet applications 7.6 4.0
IT services and consulting 1.9 40.1
Software 0.0 4.5

Source: MarketVector Indices. Data as of 3/12/2026. Index composition is not directly representative of fund holdings. Not intended as recommendations or investment advice.

Positioning with DGIN

India's domestic digital economy is underpinned by structural rather than cyclical forces: a young and expanding consumer base, growing digital infrastructure, a deepening capital market, and an accelerating pipeline of domestically focused companies entering public markets. We believe the index methodology update repositions DGIN to capture that opportunity more directly.

DGIN offers a single-ticker vehicle for investors seeking exposure to India's domestic digital growth across connectivity, fintech, e-commerce, and consumer platforms.

Important Disclosures

1 Morgan Stanley Research

2 Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI)

3 NSE, AMFI

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

An investment in the VanEck Digital India ETF (DGIN) may be subject to risks which include, but are not limited to, special risk considerations of investing in Indian issuers, equity securities, small- and medium-capitalization companies, communication services sector, information technology sector, emerging market issuers, foreign securities, foreign currency, cash transactions, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount and liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified and index- related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Small- and medium capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of a Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

© Van Eck Securities Corporation, Distributor, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Van Eck Associates Corporation.

data.title: Walmart: Mounting Headwinds And High Valuation Warrant A Downgrade
data.description:
Five months have already passed since the publication of my previous coverage of Walmart Inc. (WMT). It has already increased to its one-year high of $134 and delivered 24% returns, which justifies my buy rating despite some doubts about its valuation. But today, mounting cost pressures highlight its overpricing, especially if you look at its price multiples. WMT remains fundamentally resilient, but it's still expensive to enter or add a new position in this stock in the current market environment. Technicals adhere to it as selling pressures reveal its weakening momentum and increasing bearish tendencies.

Q4 2026: Growth and Margins Are Stable Amid Mounting Challenges

The macroeconomic landscape remains volatile and highly challenging amid stubborn inflation, new tariff woes, and intense competition. Higher cost pressures and softening demand are evident across industries, especially retailers and consumer discretionary businesses. Yet, Walmart, Inc. continues to prove why it is still part of the cream of the crop with its strategic pricing and cost management to sustain its growth. This was evident in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2026, its operating revenue reached $190.7B, up by 5.6% YoY from $180.6B. This YoY increase was higher than in my previous coverage at 4.8%, which shows sustained growth. In fact, this was the highest YoY revenue increase in four quarters, which means that it was its strongest performance in FY 2026. This was impressive considering stubborn inflation that affects consumer spending. But of course, this should not be entirely surprising since WMT has been capitalizing on cheaper pricing to attract demand, so volume can offset pricing. And as you can see, this strategy continues to work to its advantage. All its business segments had higher revenues, showing its strong domestic and international market positioning. Membership and other income increased in line with its solid customer base.

Likewise, the operating costs and expenses increased but remained more manageable. This shows its efficiency amid the increased operating capacity. With that, its operating margin also remained stable at 4.6% versus 4.3% YoY.

Considerations

Inflation remains a challenge even for a giant like WMT. After all, it capitalizes on cheaper pricing strategies to maintain demand. Aside from that, inflation affects its operating costs and expenses. The good news is that revenue growth still offsets the increase in costs and expenses to stabilize margins. My only concern is that the margin is still quite too low for its size despite having its size. It also owns a logistics and supply chain network, which should have already raised it by now. This can be a bit risky in its liquidity if it chooses to expand further or even maintain its current capacity. Also, it may only take so many discounts and promos, especially now that prices are still increasing. Note that it may also have to raise wages so its employees can keep up with inflation.

On the other hand, Amazon (AMZN) has 10.5% in operating margin. Of course, I understand that AMZN has limited brick-and-mortar presence as it heavily concentrates on e-commerce. However, it has some structural strengths that make it more competitive. For instance, it has AWS that allows it to be more efficient with its pricing and inventory management. It also generates revenue from this software since other large companies use it. Additionally, its platform is more flexible to inflation. So, it can easily adjust prices if inflation worsens amid the skyrocketing oil prices. Consumers may shift to it as it becomes relatively cheaper and more convenient. Meanwhile, Target (TGT) has 4.9% despite having a smaller size and market coverage. It appears a bit more shielded from inflation since its target market is typically middle- and upper-middle-income consumers or households.

It must also watch out for oil prices because this will most likely fuel prices. And fuel is crucial in large and asset-heavy sectors like shipping, logistics, industrial, and manufacturing. With that, there may be a domino effect on domestic prices. It may exacerbate inflation, which may squeeze the purchasing power of consumers and even WMT's pricing flexibility. Consumers may shift to e-commerce because ordering online may become cheaper than driving or commuting to the nearest Walmart store.

Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, I believe that WMT will survive.

One aspect is its business model. With its Walmart+, members may continue to enjoy discounts and perks like free delivery and early access to the newest deals. This is a combination of pricing strategies and value propositions to maintain a solid customer base. This is supported by its partnership with some brands. An example is its Burger King Savings, which allows members to enjoy 25% off on their select purchases on its app. Video streaming deals on Paramount+ make it similar to AMZN with Amazon Prime.

Another aspect is having its own logistics network instead of getting third-party services. Of course, it will incur higher fuel expenses, but this protects it from potential supply chain disruptions if many third-party freight and logistics businesses shut down amid the rising oil prices. This has high upfront costs, but this also allows it to save more in the long run. Lastly, this helps its e-commerce exposure become more streamlined and competitive, although AMZN still dominates it.

Lastly, WMT stays liquid despite its capital-intensive business. It still has enough cash and cash equivalents. This is enough to cover all borrowings in the current liabilities. It also maintains its prudent debt management considering the decent maturity profile. Note that over 80% of the total will not mature within one year. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of only 0.7x, which means that WMT only needs 0.7 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. This also shows that it still has very low leverage, making it very liquid to sustain its capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its stock price, I decided to use P/E. As you can see, the stock now trades at 44.22x, which is very high relative to the five-year average of 32.63x. If we use this to estimate its fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $89.09. To be honest, I don't think it's necessary to compare its price multiple to previous years because the fact that it's already trading above 40x says a lot about its valuation.

The Dividend Discount Model also says the same thing. Its annualized dividend of $0.99 is based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.2475. The dividend growth rate using the three-year CAGR is 7.4%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 6.0% to be more conservative than the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, I estimated its beta at 0.70, which means that WMT is less volatile than the S&P 500. Using all this information, the TP is $89.51, which agrees with my P/E TP and proves its overpricing.

Technicals

WMT's technicals have shown bearish signals in the past two months amid the increasing selling pressures. It's not yet confirmed since it hasn't broken the key support level of $115 yet. However, the decreasing trend of its price highs and lows shows increasing selling momentum. Note that the price trend has already crossed below the 0-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is already weaker than the average in the past 50 days.

Its MACD and RSI show the same thing as their downward trends persist. The MACD line is decreasing below the signal line, which means that selling volume keeps increasing. Meanwhile, the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Lastly, RSI is still decreasing, which was mainly due to the overbuying last month that prompted profit-taking. Right now, it is still above the oversold region, so the decreasing trend may continue.

Conclusion

Walmart is still a strong retailer with its stable growth and margins, supported by its solid business model and robust fundamentals. However, it must be aware of the intensifying market conditions, which may hurt its coming performance. Its valuation is also high at this level, so entering is not advisable anymore. Technicals adhere as bearish signals emerge and become more evident. I'm downgrading my rating on this stock from buy to hold.

data.title: German Ifo Index Takes A Nose Dive In March
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Germany’s long-awaited cyclical upswing took a hit in March, as the war in the Middle East has blasted away optimism. This is at least what the just-released Ifo index is telling us. Coming from the highest level since last summer, Germany’s most prominent leading indicator took a severe hit as the war in the Middle East, soaring energy prices, and new uncertainty dented previous optimism. The headline reading came in at 86.4, from 88.4 in February. While the current assessment component remained unchanged, expectations took the worst hit since the Russian invasion in Ukraine, dropping to 86.0 from 90.2 in February.

Soaring energy prices are clear risk to outlook but fiscal stimulus story still holds (for now)

The war in the Middle East has changed a lot, but not everything. The surge in energy prices could dampen any industrial rebound, even if German corporates appear relatively shielded from oil price risks for now. The effects would nonetheless leave broader marks, at least in the short term.

A first inflation wave is already in the making, from higher gasoline prices to knock-on effects on transportation and food. And longer-term implications on supply chains can no longer be excluded. Gas reserves are currently at their lowest level at this time of the year in five years, which could bring a cost push for corporates and households next winter. Let’s not forget that the so-called energy-intensive industries account for some 17% of industrial gross value added and employ just under one million people. in Germany. Still, this is not (yet) 2022, when surging energy prices – amplified by fiscal stimulus during the pandemic – first fuelled an inflation wave and then a wage-price spiral.

That said, it would be premature to drift into outright pessimism. Let’s not forget that the fundamental drivers of Germany’s economic rebound this year are still there: fiscal stimulus of more than €200bn for defence and infrastructure this year alone remains a strong argument against premature doom-mongering.

Economy still in need of reform

The first three months of the year, with geopolitical tensions, new tariff uncertainty and now soaring energy prices, are another painful reminder that Germany needs to start doing some serious multitasking. The country must develop a credible long-term plan to structurally strengthen the economy while at the same time managing successive crises.

Doing the latter without the former simply wastes time. And in this respect, the latest developments in Berlin are hardly encouraging. Hopes that the window for reform would finally open after the recent regional elections – and remain open until the next round in the autumn – have already been dashed. According to news reports, the government is now aiming to agree and implement reforms between Easter and the summer.

What kind of reforms? Healthcare and pensions are on top of the priority list, as rising costs linked to an ageing population demand urgent action. But there should be more. Germany is still missing a long-term, consistent strategy for affordable energy. Reform of the tax system could also boost growth. And the agreed reforms on deregulation, cutting bureaucracy, accelerating digitalisation and reforming the federal system still await implementation. An impressive long to-do list and a small window of opportunity, which hasn’t even started and seems to be shrinking by the day.

For now, the war in the Middle East is a risk for Germany's cyclical rebound but not (yet) enough to completely derail it, rather delay it.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Microsoft: Fortress Balance Sheet, Deep Value
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

There have been countless great American companies that found their start in a garage.

Apple (AAPL) was one company that started out of the garage of a single person's home. Microsoft (MSFT) started in a garage as well. From musical bands to massive software and computer companies, garages have been home to great excellence. Of course, there have been many great failures that have come from garages as well. Perhaps your garage band never made it to a record label deal, even though other garage bands have.

One aspect of America that I love is the ability for anyone with enough of a dream, enough of a desire to push that dream forward, and a good enough dream to come from the humblest beginnings and start something that truly turns into a powerhouse. The original founder will not be the long-term runner of the company. We see many small startups that get bought out for massive valuations because the idea is compelling enough that a bigger competitor decides to take it over. When we look at consumables, PepsiCo (PEP) bought out a prebiotic soda brand, Poppi, because it wanted to include its product type into its overall suite of products. It was a good idea, and a larger company acquired it, yielding significant returns for the founders.

As an investor, I don't try to gamble on which garage startup will be the best in the future. If I could travel back in time, I would most definitely buy the companies that I knew were going to see explosive share value growth, even if the company underneath wasn't of substantial value or worth. But I am not a time traveler; I am a humble investor. This means I will look at the market with a perspective of today, understanding history, and understanding the forecast to decide what to buy. I love to buy companies that are on sale, whose value is cheaper relative to their history and to their long-term value. The recent SaaS panic sell-off and growth rotation to value have created some substantially deeper value opportunities to buy. Today I want to cover one of those.

Let's dive in!

Long-Term Trends Are Powering Dividend Hikes

Microsoft is a juggernaut, with balanced streams of revenue. The investment thesis is similar to when we covered it last December.

Many of us use its productivity and business process products each day in the workplace (Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, LinkedIn, and Copilot). Our operating systems run on Windows. On the entertainment side, there's Xbox hardware/software and Minecraft.

Underpinning it all is the company's cloud computing network stretching over 165,000 miles. This connects the data centers to the customers, processing the trillions of requests from Bing, Xbox, LinkedIn, etc.

The big picture remains that the world is going to become more dependent on technology over the long term. That thesis was further reinforced with MSFT's latest quarterly results shared in late January.

In Q2 2026, MSFT's total revenue surged 16.7% higher year-over-year to $81.27 billion (+15% in constant currency). This topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $1.02 billion. Strength in Productivity and Business Processes (+16% reported revenue and +14% in constant currency) and Intelligent Cloud (+29% reported revenue and +28% in constant currency) offset weakness in the More Personal Computing segment (-3% reported revenue) for the quarter.

MSFT's non-GAAP diluted EPS vaulted 23.6% higher over the year-ago period to $4.14 in the fiscal second quarter. That came in $0.22 above the analyst consensus during the quarter. This was made possible by a 200 basis point expansion in the non-GAAP net profit margin to 38% for the quarter.

The future also remains bright for MSFT. Perhaps the most impressive takeaway from the Q2 2026 Earnings Call was the fact that the company's commercial remaining performance obligation soared 110% year-over-year to $625 billion.

That's why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that MSFT's non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to compound by 16.8% annually through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $13.64.

As of Dec. 31, 2025, the company's net cash and cash equivalents/marketable securities position was $49.2 billion. As the only tech company with a perfect AAA S&P credit rating, MSFT's balance sheet is a true fortress.

Its 0.9% dividend yield isn't far off the S&P 500 Index's (SP500) 1.2% yield. Best of all, the non-GAAP diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the low-20% range for FY 2026. This should allow MSFT to build on its 21-year dividend growth streak with more 10% annual payout raises (for a Chowder number of roughly 11).

Furthermore, MSFT is consistently buying back its common shares, reducing its overall share count. This helps EPS to grow when earnings continue to perform well but also allows them to have more room to grow their dividend in the future. Buybacks are extremely attractive when a company's share price is cheap when compared to its history, and at this time there is no argument that MSFT is expensive.

From a valuation standpoint, MSFT looks to be quite compelling. At the current $383, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 24.27. Put into perspective, this is far below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 29, according to FAST Graphs.

Looking ahead, we think that a reasonable fair value multiple for MSFT is 28. Rather than any deterioration in the fundamentals, such a fair value P/E ratio reflects a modest degree of conservatism on our part.

MSFT's 17.3% annual non-GAAP diluted EPS growth consensus is comparable to the 10-year CAGR of 17.9%. Throw in a flawless AAA S&P credit rating and strong profit margins, and that's why we believe this multiple can be defended.

If MSFT lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 36% total return through March 2027. By the end of June 2031, it could deliver 13.5% annual total returns.

Of course, MSFT still faces risks to its investment thesis. Relevant risks include its capex surge and regulatory scrutiny.

In FY 2026, MSFT's capex is expected to top $100 billion. In Q2 2026 alone, the company allocated a record $29.9 billion to capex (more than its annual spending in all of FY 2023).

The risk is that the majority of spending (about two-thirds in Q2 2026) is on short-lived assets (GPUs/servers), which creates very high depreciation expenses. If revenue growth doesn't remain exceptionally strong, this could weigh on profit margins.

The good news is that even with these heavy investments, MSFT's FCF per share is expected to hold pretty steady in FY 2026 at $9.43. That means it's not weakening its AAA-rated balance sheet to fund these investments.

A similar risk on this front is the fact that MSFT disclosed that about 45% of its commercial RPO balance is from OpenAI. While the remaining balance grew at a 28% clip, the immediate concern is the potential that this customer may not be able to fulfill these substantial financial obligations.

Another risk to MSFT is the ongoing probe by the Federal Trade Commission examining whether the company is illegally leveraging its enterprise software market dominance to force customers into its cloud software and Copilot services. Recently, the FTC sent queries to competitors to gauge how the alleged pricing discrepancy in running Microsoft Windows Server licenses on other platforms versus Azure is impacting their growth.

If this ultimately results in a forced price parity ruling, customers could effectively run MSFT's software on a rival cloud provider for the same price as on Azure. This could at least somewhat diminish Azure's market share gains in the future.

Conclusion

MSFT is one of those companies that, when the SaaS Apocalypse started and the rotation from growth to value picked up, I was surprised to see it sell off so heavily. I like to refer to Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and Apple as "Elder Growth." The reason I called them elder growth is that, unlike the new young startups that are growth investments seeing double-digit EPS growth but are living off negative cash flow, debt, and equity to try and grow to be of substantial size, this trio of companies are well-established. They may see strong EPS growth, but they have expansive cash flow. There are other elder growth investments that are massively cash flow positive companies, either because they're asset-light or because they have established strong market positions within their sector.

If you're looking to find a strong value argument for a growth investment in 2026, it must be cash flow positive, especially if it suffered a sell-off like we've seen across the entire sector. For years, market prognosticators have been declaring that growth names are extremely overvalued and are due for a sell-off. 2026 provided us with that. But now many of these have seen their valuations cool off to the point where they're becoming deep value opportunities compared to overheated value investments. We've seen the pendulum swing from one extreme to the other when it comes to value and growth names.

Now, value names are not growth-esque in their EPS growth but are overly expensive in their value proposition. Meanwhile, growth names are still expected to see strong growth but are actually cheap compared to their historical valuations. As a long-term investor who looks to maximize his returns from both capital gains and dividends that are growing, buying high-quality growth investments that are on sale is precisely what I'm going to do to see both the returns from the change in value proposition and also the dividend growth that is going to come with it.

At the end of the day, beating the market doesn't mean following it. It means moving ahead of it and finding value where others find fear. A king doesn't live among the peasants. He moves ahead of them and leads them. Become a Dividend King and move ahead of the market.
